content.text: Choosing between open-weight models (where the weights are publicly available for self-hosting) and closed proprietary models (accessed exclusively via vendor API) is a policy decision with implications across security, cost, compliance, and operations. This sub-subtopic provides a policy framework for making this choice systematically rather than opportunistically.

An open-weight vs closed-model sourcing policy defines the conditions under which each model category is appropriate for enterprise agentic use. Open-weight models (Meta Llama, Mistral, Falcon) are models whose weights are published under licences that permit self-hosting and often fine-tuning. Closed proprietary models (GPT-4o, Claude, Gemini) are accessible only via vendor APIs and their weights are not disclosed. A sourcing policy specifies: which data classification tiers may use open-weight self-hosted models, which require closed vendor APIs, when fine-tuning is permitted, and how each category is managed in the approved model registry.

The open-weight ecosystem has matured significantly: recent open-weight models achieve performance comparable to frontier closed models on many enterprise tasks, at a fraction of the cost for high-volume workloads. Enterprises that default to closed models for all use cases incur unnecessary cost and vendor concentration risk. Conversely, enterprises that adopt open-weight models without assessing the operational and safety requirements introduce infrastructure complexity and potential safety gaps. A policy provides the governance structure to make the right choice for each use case rather than defaulting to one category for all.
inference cost is the infrastructure (GPU) cost, not per-token pricing. At high volume (>100M tokens/day), open-weight can be 3–10x cheaper.
prompts never leave your infrastructure; no vendor DPA required for inference.
full weight access enables custom fine-tuning on proprietary data.
model weights are portable; you can switch hosting providers.
models can be deployed in air-gapped environments where no internet connectivity is available.
requires GPU capacity management, model serving software (vLLM, TGI), and MLOps expertise.
community-released models vary in safety fine-tuning quality; base models (unaligned) are not suitable for production.
open-weight licences vary widely (Apache 2.0, LLAMA Community Licence); some restrict commercial use or revenue above a threshold.
you own the infrastructure; uptime is your responsibility.
model serving software has CVEs; you are responsible for patching.
vendor operates serving, scaling, and patching.
frontier closed models have extensive safety fine-tuning backed by vendor commitments.
uptime and latency SLAs with financial remedies.
frontier model improvements are immediately accessible without redeployment.
economically inefficient for very high-volume batch workloads.
capability and pricing changes are at vendor discretion.
data may leave your jurisdiction without careful DPA and residency configuration.

Open-weight models carry a different risk profile than closed APIs. With open-weight, your primary risk is internal: misuse, fine-tuning instability, or inadvertent release of proprietary weights. With closed models, risks are external: vendor price increases, deprecation of a model version, or outage in the provider's inference endpoint. Enterprises should map workloads to risk tolerance rather than defaulting to one category.

Closed-model TCO is dominated by token costs at scale. Open-weight TCO is dominated by infrastructure, engineering, and operational overhead—a GPU cluster for inference, monitoring, autoscaling, and an on-call rotation. For workloads processing fewer than ten million tokens per month, closed APIs typically win on TCO. Above one hundred million tokens per month, a well-run open-weight deployment often becomes cost competitive, especially when spot instances or reserved capacity are factored in.
prompt tokens, completion tokens, context window size, request volume
GPU instance type, utilization rate, storage, DevOps headcount
highly workload-dependent; model a minimum of three scenarios before deciding
egress fees, rate-limit retries, vendor lock-in switching costs

Certain regulated industries face constraints that push them toward open-weight models regardless of cost. Healthcare and financial services organizations subject to HIPAA, SOC 2 Type II, or PCI-DSS must control where inference runs, which data reaches third-party endpoints, and how logs are retained. Open-weight deployment on private infrastructure satisfies these constraints more directly. However, closed providers increasingly offer BAA-covered, VPC-isolated endpoints, reducing this gap.

If your use case requires task-specific fine-tuning—domain adaptation, instruction style alignment, or output format conditioning—open-weight models give you full control over the training pipeline. Closed models offer parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) through vendor APIs on a limited basis, but access to the base weights for full fine-tuning is not available. Enterprises with proprietary datasets that represent genuine competitive differentiation should weight open-weight models more heavily in their sourcing policy.

Closed-model providers deprecate model versions on their own schedules. When GPT-4 Turbo Preview is retired and replaced, your outputs may shift without your consent. For audit-sensitive applications—legal review, compliance checks, or financial reporting—reproducibility of outputs over time is a contractual and operational requirement. Open-weight models stored in your registry guarantee that the exact weights used to produce a decision on a given date remain accessible for re-execution or audit replay.

attrs.alt: Open-weight vs closed-model decision framework: key factors compared

content.text: Data classification: Highly Restricted (cannot leave premises)
Open-weight (economics), or closed with high-volume commit
Fine-tuning on proprietary domain data required
When establishing the initial AI sourcing policy for a new enterprise AI programme.
When a team proposes using an open-weight model in a new workflow.
When evaluating cost reduction opportunities for existing high-volume agent workflows.
When data residency requirements preclude sending data to a vendor API.
Define the enterprise's current MLOps capability level: no capability, emerging, or mature.
For each active or planned agent workflow, assess: data classification, daily token volume, latency requirement, and fine-tuning need.
Apply the policy decision matrix to each workflow to determine the recommended model category.
For open-weight selections: assess licence compliance, identify the hosting infrastructure, and complete a safety alignment review of the specific model version.
Update the approved model registry with open-weight entries using T5 tier for on-premise or self-hosted models.
Publish the policy and decision matrix to the developer portal so teams can self-serve the decision before submitting a registry request.

A logistics company analysed its three agentic workflows: a real-time route optimisation agent (interactive latency, 8M tokens/day, confidential data), a nightly shipment anomaly detection batch (batch latency, 180M tokens/day, internal data), and an air-gapped ops planning tool (on-premise required, 2M tokens/day). Applying the policy matrix, it selected a closed model for real-time routing (latency SLA) and the air-gapped ops tool (on-premise open-weight). For the anomaly detection batch, it deployed a fine-tuned Llama model self-hosted on GPU cloud, reducing the per-token cost by 6x versus the closed API and saving $280k annually.
Ignoring licence terms for open-weight models:
some open-weight licences restrict commercial use above a certain revenue threshold or require disclosure of derivative models.
GPU infrastructure, MLOps engineering, and model serving maintenance have real costs; model the full TCO.
Using base (unaligned) open-weight models in production:
base models without safety fine-tuning are not suitable for production agentic use; only use instruction-tuned and safety-aligned variants.
vLLM and TGI have security vulnerabilities like any software; include them in your security patching programme.
Model the full TCO for both options at 12 and 36 months before committing to either for a new workflow.
For open-weight models, pin to a specific model version (including fine-tune checkpoint) and test before upgrading.
Maintain parity evaluations: run both open-weight and closed candidates on your task benchmark annually to validate the category choice is still correct as capabilities evolve.
Document licence compliance for each open-weight model: record the licence type, applicable restrictions, and legal approval.

Self-hosted open-weight models scale through horizontal GPU deployment. At moderate scale (10–50M tokens/day), a single 8-GPU A100 server running vLLM can serve most workloads. At high scale (>200M tokens/day), Kubernetes-based auto-scaling of multiple GPU nodes is required. Monitor GPU utilisation, KV cache hit rate, and queue depth as primary capacity indicators. Closed models scale automatically via the vendor's infrastructure—the enterprise's responsibility is rate limit management, not infrastructure scaling.
Hybrid deployment: open-weight for batch, closed for real-time

Many enterprises adopt a hybrid model: open-weight self-hosted models for high-volume, latency-tolerant batch workloads and closed vendor APIs for low-volume, interactive real-time agents. This hybrid maximises cost efficiency while preserving the SLA guarantees and operational simplicity of closed APIs for customer-facing use cases. The model abstraction layer is essential for a hybrid deployment: agent code that calls a unified LLMClient interface can route to self-hosted vLLM or a closed vendor API without code changes.
High-performance open-weight model serving; OpenAI-compatible API
Text Generation Inference; enterprise-grade serving with quantisation
Developer-friendly local model serving; not production-grade at scale
Model abstraction layer that unifies open-weight and closed-model APIs
Production inference serving with GPU batching and multiple backends
simpler governance; higher cost at volume; vendor lock-in risk.
maximum data control; requires significant MLOps investment; may lack frontier reasoning capabilities.
optimises cost and capability by applying each category where it has the strongest advantage.

Open-weight self-hosted models shift security responsibility entirely to the enterprise. The model serving API surface must be secured with authentication (API keys or OAuth), network access controls (private VPC only), TLS termination, and input/output logging. The model weights themselves should be stored with integrity verification (hash checks at load time) to detect unauthorised modification. Closed-model security is vendor-managed, but prompt injection risks exist in both deployment models—implement input sanitisation and output validation regardless of model category.
Apply a decision matrix based on data classification, token volume, latency requirement, and MLOps capability—not just cost or capability.
Highly restricted data requires self-hosted open-weight models; no data must leave the enterprise boundary.
Model the full TCO (GPU infrastructure + MLOps + patching) for open-weight options—they are not free.
Use only safety-aligned instruction-tuned variants of open-weight models; base models are not production-safe.
Adopt a hybrid approach: open-weight for high-volume batch, closed for interactive real-time agents.
